exemplar.title: A reconstruction of Loftus and Palmer (1974) to investigate the effect of Reconstructive Memory and Schema Theory

detailedExemplarData.title: A reconstruction of Loftus and Palmer (1974) to investigate the effect of Reconstructive Memory and Schema Theory

subject.title: Psychology
subject.description:
IB Psychology is a Group 3 subject in the International Baccalaureate Diploma Programme (IBDP). The course examines human behavior and mental processes through scientific inquiry, exploring biological, cognitive, and sociocultural approaches to understanding behavior. Students develop critical thinking skills while investigating contemporary psychological research, research methods, and ethical considerations in psychological practice.

The course is available at Standard Level (SL, 150 teaching hours) and Higher Level (HL, 240 hours), with first assessment in 2027.

Assessment consists of Paper 1 (integration of concepts, content, and contexts through short-answer and extended-response questions: SL 2 hours, HL 2 hours, SL 50%, HL 40%), Paper 2 (application of psychological knowledge to research contexts: SL 1 hour, HL 2 hours, SL 25%, HL 20%), Paper 3 (HL only: research methods and data analysis, 1 hour, 20%), and Internal Assessment (research proposal development: SL 25%, HL 20%).

strengths: Clear, focused aim linked to Loftus & Palmer replication and schema theory
Both null and research hypotheses are fully operationalised with precise IV and DV definitions
Theory of reconstructive memory and schema theory are accurately described and connected to the investigation
weaknesses: Original Loftus & Palmer statistical results are incomplete, cutting off mid-description
Minor phrasing and citation inconsistencies (e.g., use of 'effect to' vs 'effect on', inconsistent hypothesis formatting)
The theoretical discussion, while accurate, could be deepened with additional empirical support or citations
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Although this section meets most top-band descriptorsâclearly stated aim with relevance, well-described theory/model, fully operationalised variables and hypothesesâminor omissions (incomplete summary of original study, citation/style inconsistencies) prevent a higher mark. A score of 4 reflects strong achievement with room for enhanced depth and precision.

strengths: Independent-measures design is not only described but justified in terms of order-effect and demand-characteristic control
Sampling technique and participant characteristics are stated with justification of convenience and motivation
weaknesses: Explanation of controlled variables and materials is present but thin, lacking deeper rationale or discussion of potential limitations (e.g., paper questionnaires)
No discussion of how sampling constraints might affect external validity
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: This criterion demonstrates clear description and some explanation (design and sampling meet top band), but the rationale for materials and control of extraneous variables is underdeveloped. A mid-band score of 2/4 reflects solid reporting but limited explanatory depth.

strengths: Appropriate inferential test (Mann-Whitney) and descriptive statistics are applied with overall correct interpretation
Findings are linked back to the hypothesis, noting trend direction and role of the outlier removal
weaknesses: Box-and-whisker plot axes are unlabeled and scaling is compressed, impairing clarity
Inconsistencies in reported medians between text and table and lack of formal outlier criterion weaken statistical rigor
P-value reporting lacks detail (U statistic, power analysis) and graphic quality is low
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The analysis applies suitable descriptive and inferential techniques and correctly interprets findings, but presentation errors, statistical inconsistencies, and incomplete reporting limit the quality. A score of 3/6 reflects competent analysis marred by notable technical flaws.

strengths: Strengths and limitations are systematically identified and linked to aspects of validity
Proposed modifications directly address specific limitations and are justified in terms of improving methodological rigour
weaknesses: Discussion of findings with respect to schema theory is brief and lacks theoretical depth
Inconsistency in p-value reporting (contradictory statements) undermines interpretive clarity
Recommendations could be expanded with quantitative details (e.g., room setup parameters)
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: While the section effectively critiques methodological strengths/limitations and offers well-justified modifications, the theoretical evaluation is shallow and contains internal inconsistencies. A score of 3/6 appropriately balances these merits and shortcomings.

strandSelections.reasoning: A box-and-whisker plot that contrasts groups and highlights the outlier is provided, addressing the hypothesis. Yet axes are not labelled and scaling is compressed, constituting presentation errors. Hence it fits the 3-4 band but the lower score of 3 is justified.

The student explicitly states the statistic (p = 0.5 > 0.05), rejects the research hypothesis, supports the null and relates this to the data trend after removing the outlier. The linkage back to the hypothesis is clear. Interpretation is correct though concisely reasoned; one numerical slip (median) limits a perfect 6, so 5 is awarded.

Both null and research hypotheses are written. The IV (verb: smashed vs contacted) and DV (estimated speed in km/h) are **clearly operationalised**, specifying wording and measurement units, fully meeting the top-band criteria with no observable flaw.

Opportunity sampling is identified, participant numbers, gender balance and English proficiency are given, and the **choice is justified** (convenience, motivation). Although justification is brief, it meets the requirement of explanation rather than description, warranting the top band with 4 marks.

Schema theory and reconstructive memory are **accurately described** with clear reference to top-down processing and the way leading questions alter recall. The link to the present investigation is made explicit (âschemas can be manipulated â¦ hence we replicate Loftus & Palmerâ). A concise but accurate theoretical bridge is provided, aligning with the 5-6 band; limited citation depth keeps the mark to 5 rather than 6.

Findings are revisited and **connected to schema theory and Loftus & Palmer**, but discussion remains brief and primarily descriptive, offering limited theoretical depth. This satisfies the 3-4 descriptor, but the moderate depth aligns with a 3.

The student explicitly states an aim â to reconstruct Loftus & Palmer (1974) to examine the effect of verb intensity on speed estimates â and then **explains the wider relevance** to schema theory, the fallibility of eyewitness testimony and legal practice. The relevance is not merely identified; it is coherently elaborated, satisfying the top-band descriptor. Minor brevity in societal implications prevents awarding the full 6.

Multiple strengths (motivated IB sample, independent measures, standardised instructions) and limitations (low generalisability, viewing angles, participant variables) are **explained and explicitly linked to validity**. The coverage is systematic though not exhaustive, warranting a solid 5 in the top band.

Controlled variables (identical instructions, same video, seating rules) and materials (paper questionnaires) are listed **with some rationale** (reduce distractions, confusion). The explanation is present but thinner than other strands, so a mid-band 3 within 3-4 is appropriate.

Two concrete modifications (replace âcontactedâ with âtappedâ; individual screening room) are clearly **tied to the earlier limitations** and justified in terms of improving validity. Justification is sound though limited in number, earning 5 within the top band.

The student not only describes that an independent-measures design was used but **explains why** (avoids order effects and demand characteristics). This explanatory element fulfils the highest band for Exploration (3-4). The explanation is succinct yet accurate, meriting the upper score of 4.

Descriptive statistics (mean, SD, median) and an appropriate inferential test (Mann-Whitney for independent, non-parametric data) are applied. Calculations are plausible and conclusions derived; however, reporting shows minor inconsistencies (median mis-copied; justification for outlier removal not statistically formal). These small issues place the work in the 3-4 band, at the higher score of 4.

annotations.comment: Axes of the box-and-whisker plot are unlabeled and scale markers are missing, which detracts from clarity and makes hypothesis assessment difficult.
The resolution of the boxplot image appears low; ensure high-quality graphics with clear labels to meet presentation standards.
There is an inconsistency: the text reports both group medians as 47.5, but Table 1 lists Group B median as 45; review descriptive statistics for accuracy.
Removing the outlier is justified qualitatively; include a formal outlier criterion (e.g., Tukey's fences) to strengthen the statistical justification.
Report the MannâWhitney U statistic alongside the p-value (e.g., $$U=... , p=0.5$$) and discuss the test's power to provide a fuller inferential analysis.

The description of schema theory is thorough and establishes foundational concepts clearly; consider integrating a citation to Bartlett (1932) or more recent empirical support to strengthen the theoretical grounding.
The discussion of processing modes (top-down, pattern recognition, stereotyping) is accurate; however, clarify how each mode directly relates to reconstructive memory in eyewitness testimony.
The summary of the original Loftus and Palmer (1974) results is cut off mid-sentence; ensure the complete statistical outcomes and effect sizes are included for accuracy.
The aim is clearly stated and relevance to legal practice and personal vulnerability is well explained; consider expanding on specific societal implications for eyewitness procedures.
The independent and dependent variables are identified but could be operationalized more precisely here by stating the measurement unit (e.g., km/h) to meet full operationalisation criteria.
The null hypothesis correctly states the comparison but use 'effect on' instead of 'effect to' for standard scientific phrasing.
The research hypothesis is clear and directional; consider formatting Hâ consistently using LaTeX, e.g. $$H_1$$, to enhance clarity in presentation.
The rationale for using an independent measures design is well explained, demonstrating how it mitigates order effects and demand characteristics.
The choice of opportunity sampling is justified with reference to convenience and participant motivation, demonstrating an understanding of sampling constraints.
Including ethical procedures (consent and debriefing) strengthens the study; consider referring to specific ethical guidelines (e.g., APA Code) for completeness.
Controlled variables and materials are described with reasoning to reduce distractions and confusion; consider discussing potential limitations of using paper questionnaires.
Decimal commas in table entries (e.g., 55,5) may conflict with IB formatting guidelines; use decimal points (e.g., 55.5) for consistency.
The connection to reconstructive memory theory is stated but lacks depth; expand on how non-significant results inform or challenge schema theory predictions.
The explanation of verb precision affecting variability is insightful; consider linking this directly to statistical dispersion measures for stronger theoretical evaluation.
The discussion of sample strength and generalizability is clear; add a note on demographic limitations (e.g., age range) to contextualize external validity.
You correctly identify generalizability as a limitation, but consider proposing targeted populations for future sampling to offset this.
Highlighting participant memory capacity as an extraneous variable is valuable; recommending memory screening tasks could strengthen internal validity modifications.
The material setup strengths and viewing-angle limitation are well described; quantify the potential impact (e.g., degrees of view variance) to inform modification feasibility.
The proposed verb 'tapped' modification directly addresses the limitation of connotative variability; excellent linkage of modification to a specific limitation.
Isolating participants in identical viewing rooms is a sound modification; consider detailing room setup parameters to ensure replicability.
The conclusion states 'p < 0.05' which contradicts earlier 'p > 0.05'; correct this inconsistency to maintain result integrity.
